nodes.label: Formation of Contract
nodes.summary: Agreement (Offer and Acceptance): A contract is a legally enforceable agreement, and the existence of an agreement is determined objectively based on what a reasonable person would conclude from the parties' words and actions. Offer vs. Invitation to Treat: An offer must be distinguished from an invitation to treat; for example, the display of goods with a price tag or an advertisement is generally merely an invitation to treat, not a binding offer.

nodes.label: Communication of Acceptance
nodes.summary: Acceptance must generally be communicated to the offeror, but under the "postal rule," acceptance by post may be effective upon mailing. For electronic communications, receipt may depend on whether an "information system" was designated under the Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553).

nodes.label: Consideration
nodes.summary: A fundamental requirement for all contracts not made under seal is consideration, which must be "sufficient" (of some value in the eyes of the law) but need not be "adequate" (of equal value). Past consideration is generally not valid consideration. nodes.text: Consideration: A fundamental requirement for all contracts not made under seal is consideration, which must be "sufficient" (of some value in the eyes of the law) but need not be "adequate" (of equal value). Past consideration is generally not valid consideration. However, the performance of an existing contractual duty may constitute valid consideration if it confers a "practical benefit" on the promisor, following the principle in Williams v Roffey Bros.

nodes.label: Intention to Create Legal Relations
nodes.summary: For an agreement to be enforceable, the parties must intend to create legal relations, which is presumed in commercial contexts but generally presumed absent in social or domestic arrangements.
nodes.text: Intention to Create Legal Relations: For an agreement to be enforceable, the parties must intend to create legal relations, which is presumed in commercial contexts but generally presumed absent in social or domestic arrangements.

nodes.label: Capacity
nodes.summary: Exceptions to full contractual capacity include minors, drunkards, and those with mental incapacity. Contracts for "necessaries" or beneficial contracts of employment are enforceable against minors, who must pay a reasonable price for them.

nodes.label: Formality
nodes.summary: The general rule is that contracts can be made in any form, including orally. A major exception is contracts for the sale or disposition of land, which must be in writing or evidenced by a signed memorandum under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219), subject to the equitable doctrine of part performance. Guarantees no longer need to be in writing under Hong Kong law.

nodes.label: Contractual Terms
nodes.summary: Express Terms and the Parol Evidence Rule: Where parties have reduced their agreement to writing, the parol evidence rule generally prevents the admission of extrinsic or oral evidence to add to, vary, or contradict the written terms. Implied Terms: Terms may be implied by custom, in law, by statute, or in fact to give "business efficacy" to the contract or because they are so obvious they "go without saying" (the officious bystander test).

nodes.label: Statutory Implied Terms
nodes.summary: The Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) implies conditions that the seller has the right to sell, and that goods will correspond with their description, be of merchantable quality, and be fit for purpose. The Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457) implies terms that services will be carried out with reasonable care and skill, within a reasonable time, and for a reasonable charge.

nodes.label: Conditions
nodes.summary: Essential terms; any breach entitles the innocent party to terminate the contract and claim damages.

nodes.label: Warranties
nodes.summary: Lesser terms; breach entitles the innocent party only to claim damages, not to terminate.

nodes.label: Innominate (Intermediate) Terms
nodes.summary: The right to terminate depends on whether the consequences of the breach deprive the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract.

nodes.label: Exemption Clauses
nodes.summary: Clauses attempting to exclude or restrict liability are construed strictly against the party relying on them (the contra proferentem rule).
nodes.text: Exemption Clauses: Clauses attempting to exclude or restrict liability are construed strictly against the party relying on them (the contra proferentem rule).

nodes.label: Statutory Control of Exemption Clauses
nodes.summary: Under the Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71), a party cannot exclude liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. Excluding other negligence liability, or liability for breach of contract when dealing on standard terms or with a consumer, is only valid if the clause satisfies the statutory test of "reasonableness".

nodes.label: Vitiating Factors
nodes.summary: Misrepresentation: A false statement of fact that induces a party to enter into a contract renders the contract voidable. Remedies include rescission and damages under the Misrepresentation Ordinance (Cap. 284), which allows damages for negligent misrepresentation unless the representor proves they had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true. Mistake: A contract may be void if it is affected by an operative mistake, including common mistake (both parties make the same fundamental mistake), mutual mistake (parties are at cross-purposes), or unilateral mistake (one party is mistaken as to the terms or identity, and the other knows it).

nodes.label: Duress
nodes.summary: Illegitimate pressure, including economic duress, that coerces the will of a party and leaves them with no practical alternative makes the contract voidable.

nodes.label: Undue Influence
nodes.summary: An equitable doctrine rendering a contract voidable where consent is obtained through unacceptable influence; it can be actual or presumed from a relationship of trust and confidence (e.g., parent/child, solicitor/client) where the transaction calls for an explanation.

nodes.label: Unconscionability
nodes.summary: The Unconscionable Contracts Ordinance (Cap. 458) empowers courts to refuse to enforce, or to revise, unconscionable terms in contracts where one party deals as a consumer for goods or services. At common law, unconscionability applies sparingly where a party in a superior bargaining position abuses that position to produce an agreement with severe unfairness.

nodes.label: Illegality
nodes.summary: Contracts that are illegal as formed (e.g., agreements to commit a crime, or unauthorized gambling under the Gambling Ordinance) or contrary to public policy are generally void and unenforceable.

nodes.label: Termination and Discharge
nodes.summary: By Performance: The general rule requires exact and precise performance of contractual obligations. By Breach: A repudiatory breach, or a breach of a condition, gives the innocent party an "election": they may choose to affirm the contract or accept the repudiation to terminate the contract and claim damages.

nodes.label: By Frustration
nodes.summary: Occurs when a supervening, unforeseeable event, occurring without the fault of either party, makes performance impossible or radically transforms the nature of the contractual obligations. The Law Amendment and Reform (Consolidation) Ordinance (Cap. 23) grants courts discretion to adjust the financial rights and liabilities of the parties following frustration.

nodes.label: Remedies for Breach
nodes.summary: Unliquidated Damages: The primary common law remedy. The aim is compensatory: to put the innocent party in the position they would have been in had the contract been properly performed. Remoteness of Damage: Under the rule in Hadley v Baxendale, losses are only recoverable if they arise naturally in the ordinary course of things, or were reasonably foreseeable as a probable result of the breach at the time the contract was made.

nodes.label: Liquidated Damages vs. Penalties
nodes.summary: A liquidated damages clause specifying a sum payable upon breach is enforceable if it is a genuine pre-estimate of loss or protects a legitimate interest proportionally; if it is out of all proportion and penal in nature, it is an unenforceable penalty.

nodes.label: Equitable Remedies
nodes.summary: Specific performance (an order to fulfill the contract) and injunctions (an order to refrain from breaching a negative obligation) are discretionary and generally only granted when monetary damages are inadequate.

nodes.label: Restitution (Unjust Enrichment)
nodes.summary: Available exceptionally, such as when there has been a "total failure of basis" or consideration, allowing a party to recover money paid under a void or discharged contract.
nodes.text: Restitution (Unjust Enrichment): Available exceptionally, such as when there has been a "total failure of basis" or consideration, allowing a party to recover money paid under a void or discharged contract.

nodes.label: Privity of Contract
nodes.summary: General Rule: At common law, the doctrine of privity dictates that only a person who is a party to a contract can sue or be sued on it. Statutory Exceptions: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623) provides a major exception, allowing a third party to enforce a contractual term if the contract expressly provides that they may do so, or if the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party, provided the third party is expressly identified.

nodes.label: Specific Types of Contracts (Specialized Domains)
nodes.summary: Sale of Goods and Services: Contracts are governed by the Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) and the Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457), which imply strict conditions regarding title, description, merchantable quality, and reasonable care and skill. Key sub-topics include the passing of property/title, the nemo dat rule (a seller cannot pass better title than they have), and retention of title (Romalpa) clauses. Sale of Land and Leases: Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be evidenced in writing under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219), subject to the equitable doctrine of part performance. The framework should map Provisional Agreements for Sale and Purchase (PASP)—which are legally binding—and the vendor's implied duty to prove and give "good title".

nodes.label: Employment Contracts
nodes.summary: A critical distinction must be drawn between a contract of service (employer-employee, attracting vicarious liability and statutory protections) and a contract for services (independent contractor). These contracts feature an implied duty of mutual trust and confidence.

nodes.summary: Governed by the stringent duty of utmost good faith (uberrimae fidei), requiring full disclosure of material facts. The insured must have an "insurable interest" in the subject matter, distinguishing it from wagering contracts.

nodes.label: Conflict of Laws (Cross-Border Contracts)
nodes.summary: Choice of Law: If parties make an express agreement on governing law, it is generally respected. Without it, courts seek an implied choice or apply the system of law with the "closest and most real connection" to the transaction (evaluating place of performance, residence, etc.). Long-Arm Jurisdiction: Under RHC O.11, Hong Kong courts can take jurisdiction over overseas defendants if the contract was made in HK, governed by HK law, or breached in HK, subject to forum non conveniens discretion.

nodes.label: Restitution and Unjust Enrichment (Quasi-Contract)
nodes.summary: While not strictly contract law, this is the vital fallback when a contract is void, frustrated, or rescinded. The cause of action requires showing that: (1) the defendant was enriched, (2) it was at the plaintiff's expense, and (3) there is an unjust factor (such as mistake, duress, or a total failure of consideration). nodes.label: Cheng Kwei Sheng v Li Shek Tong [1976] HKCA
nodes.summary: Court of Appeal addressed the extent to which extrinsic evidence is admissible to interpret a written document. However, where evidence shows an apparent ambiguity, surrounding circumstances may be considered

nodes.label: BP Refinery (Westernport) Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings (1977)
nodes.summary: Established the five-condition test for implying terms in fact: (1) reasonable and equitable, (2) necessary to give business efficacy, (3) so obvious it "goes without saying" (officious bystander), (4) capable of clear expression, (5) not contradictory to express terms
